results.possibleIdentity: Phil Deal is a San Francisco-based artist, saxophonist, and astrologer who has been an active figure in the city's artistic community since the early 1970s. He is most notably associated with Project Artaud, a pioneering artist-run community and non-profit collective established in 1971 in a decommissioned factory. A performance space within the complex, the Phil Deal Performance Space (also known as the Phil Deal Performance Gallery), is named in his honor. Deal was also an inaugural participant in the San Francisco Open Studios, an event that allows artists to share their work and engage with the public, with records tracing its beginnings to as early as 1970. In 1996, he was identified as an artist and astrologer with an interest in creation spirituality and healing. His career spans several decades, including regular appearances at poetry and music events in San Francisco.

Peter Deal was a prominent British photographer and darkroom specialist active in the late 1960s. He trained in New York City under renowned photographers such as Irving Penn and Bert Stern. Deal is particularly noted for his work on Bert Stern's "The Last Sitting" with Marilyn Monroe (1962). He was considered a perfectionist in fashion and beauty photography and worked for major publications, including Harper's Bazaar UK, where his photographs appeared on covers and in feature spreads during the late 1960s (notably in 1965 and 1968). He was also a mentor to other photographers and was recognized as a master black-and-white printer.

Paul Deal was a scientist and engineer at the NASA Ames Research Center active during the late 1960s and 1970s. His work focused on biological experiments in space, such as studying gravitational field effects on fertilized eggs, and participating in studies regarding space resources and habitat design. Given the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.)'s specific mission to match artists with engineers and scientists, and E.A.T.'s documented involvement with NASA centers and technological projects (such as 'Projects Outside Art' in 1970), he may be the 'P. Deal' referenced as a technical collaborator. NASA records identify him as a specialist and contributor to technical papers in regenerative life-support systems and planetary biology during this period.
